feedback.text: This topic is incredibly important to me, and I believe the scope of the topic is already very good. We are on a trajectory where one of the biggest entertainment industries have taught our next generations that: 1) Seeing commercials every 30 seconds is normal and the temptation to spend more money is always around the corner, even in full-price products, 2) functional digital products are a gesture (and it is rude to complain about broken features months after full-price release), 3) that you are a sucker if you dont preorder or buy the "enhanced" version of releases, because the "standard" edition is watered down, and finally that 4) agreements are temporary because one side overwhelms with massive user/consumer-agreement yet still retain the option to alter them further, including raising prices, adding ads or discontinue services entirely, often rendering products (with unnecesary online dependecies) or "digital purchases" (that are in fact only so called "licenses" og "indefinate rentals") fully disabled. As it stands, there is a genuine likelyhood that the experiences, stories, games, shows, movies that I enjoyed as a child will not be available to share with my future children - in some cases independently of my will to pay again or tinker with old technology, because some games, tv shows, websites, webcomics and movies are simply not provided anywhere anymore, and even physical copies depend on servers that are now disabled. Also, as a very important sidenote: as the internet in general moves to prioritize tech-giants and their services, many small creators are being pushed out of our common open internet. As an example: A relative of mine has dedicated years of work to researching and hosting information about ww1 and ww2 ships, crews, prisoners of war etc. As I would call it, a hobby historian. This and much more including open knowledge sharing and entertainment will be less common in the future, because 1) independant creators are less visible (even when they dont care about revenue) as large corporations such as google prioritize their own services or websites of their business partners, and 2) corporation-dependant creators (such as youtubers) do not control their platform or audience connection, meaning if the creator falls into disfavor (legitimately or simply by spurries copyright claims with no real power to appeal) or the platform changes significantly (like transforming into a worse service, for example by increasingly requiring the user to watch obnoxous ads that are borderline scams, or chasing an increasingly singleminded algorithm to be visible), then the creator is forced to chase trends rather than their prefered topic, devote massive ressources, be invisible or quit. Thank you for finally recognizing this digital world and the need for regulations on par or beyond with the rest of our society's laws and regulations.
